article_title: MIT Uses Generative AI to Build Wireless Vision Systems That See Through Walls

content_preview.title: MIT Uses Generative AI to Build Wireless Vision Systems That See Through Walls
content_preview.summary: MIT researchers present Wave-Former and RISE, two systems that combine millimeter-wave radar with generative AI to reconstruct hidden objects and entire indoor scenes through obstructions, achieving up to twice the precision of prior methods.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at MIT's Media Lab have unveiled two systems that use generative AI and millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar to perceive objects and environments hidden behind walls, packaging, and other common obstructions. The work, [announced on March 19, 2026](https://news.mit.edu/2026/generative-ai-improves-wireless-vision-system-sees-through-obstructions-0319), will be presented at the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) and addresses a decade-old challenge...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

source_verification.https://news.mit.edu/2026/generative-ai-: Confirmed: Wave-Former reconstructed ~70 everyday objects with ~20% accuracy improvement; RISE tested on 100+ trajectories with ~2x precision; led by Fadel Adib; funding from NSF, MIT Media Lab, Amazon; CVPR presentation confirmed.
source_verification.https://techxplore.com/news/2026-03-gene: Confirmed (fetched successfully despite 403 to bots): Corroborates Wave-Former and RISE details, researcher names (Dodds, Zhou, Adib), performance metrics, and practical applications including warehouse robotics and smart home use cases.
source_verification.https://computing.mit.edu/news/generativ: Confirmed: Adib quote 'We are using AI to finally unlock wireless vision' verified; privacy advantages of mmWave over cameras confirmed; full researcher team listed; ghost signals terminology and scene reconstruction details match article.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All quantitative claims verified against sources: ~70 objects reconstructed, ~20% accuracy improvement for Wave-Former, ~2x precision for RISE, 100+ trajectories tested. Researcher names (Fadel Adib, Laura Dodds, Kaichen Zhou) and affiliations correct. Funding sources and CVPR venue confirmed.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, informative tone throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. Technical concepts (specularity, mmWave, multipath reflections) explained clearly for a general audience. The 'What This Means' section appropriately notes open questions about scaling beyond lab settings.
editor_notes.originality: Well-structured synthesis of three source articles covering the same research announcement. Adds value by clearly explaining the specularity problem and distinguishing between Wave-Former (object-level) and RISE (scene-level) approaches. No signs of excessive copying from any single source.
recommendations: techxplore.com and computing.mit.edu are reputable sources that could be added to the allowlist
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong news article covering genuine MIT research with solid sourcing. All claims verified across three independent source pages. The article accurately represents the research findings without overstating capabilities and appropriately flags privacy implications. Approved without reservations.
